The Molecular Sciences Institute

About the Institute

The Molecular Sciences Institute is dedicated to the advancement of basic biological research in a multidisciplinary academic environment. We believe that the key challenge for biological sciences in the first decade of the next millennium will be to accumulate, organize, and rigorously analyze and evaluate the complex data that will result from the current genome sequencing endeavors. To meet this challenge, we will develop technologies to accumulate higher-value types of data than those coming from conventional genomics; generate intellectual, mathematical, and computational frameworks to aid our thinking about complex biological processes; and synthesize data and frameworks to allow us to make testable predictions about the behaviors of living systems. The goal of the Institute is to weave the scientific disciplines of physics, engineering, computer science, and mathematics as integral components with biology, genetics, and chemistry in this new "Post-Genomic" biological paradigm.

Sydney Brenner

Sydney Brenner is the Director of Research and President of the Institute. He received degrees in Medicine and Science from the University of Witwatersrand, South Africa, and a D. Phil. in Chemistry from Oxford University in England. Dr. Brenner is known for his substantial contributions to genetics, including the identification of mRNA (with Francois Jacob and Matthew Meselson), the elucidation that the genetic code consists of triplets (with Francis Crick), and the development of the nematode as a model research organism. He was Director of the Unit of Molecular Genetics at the Medical Research Council in Cambridge, England, and is a Senior Fellow of King's College in Cambridge. His recent work involves the development of the compact genome of the pufferfish as a model vertebrate genome. This work is allowing the application of comparative genetics to the analysis of vertebrate gene regulation. Dr. Brenner has also been studying vertebrate gene and genome evolution. In the course of his work, he has developed new ways of analyzing gene sequence which promise to give insight into vertebrate embryonic development.

Roger Brent

Roger Brent joined the Institute as Associate Director of Research in January of 1998. He received a Bachelor's degree in Computer Science and Mathematics from the University of Southern Mississippi and a Ph.D. in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology from Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Dr. Brent was most recently an Associate Professor at Harvard Medical School and Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston. His recent work includes the development of biological technologies to map genetic networks and test functions of genes and alleles, and the generation of computational tools to help think further about this data. This research is likely to lead to changes in the way researchers test ideas about gene function and to new therapies. It may also lead to a broader understanding of the function of polymorphic alleles within the population.

Growth of the Institute

The Institute was formed in June of 1996 as an independent California not-for-profit public benefit corporation. As such, the Institute has no owners or stockholders and is governed by a self-perpetuating Board of Trustees. The Institute is tax exempt under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code and California law.

The Institute was founded with a $10 million, 5-year, "no-strings" grant from Philip Morris Companies, Inc. Additional operating funding is provided by public and private sources, including the National Institutes of General Medical Sciences (NIH), the National Human Genome Research Institute (NIH), the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, Hoechst Marion Roussel, Inc., and Lynx Therapeutics. The Institute intends to build its endowment by seeking funding from additional sources in the next several years. The Institute is dedicated to publication of its research findings in scientific journals and making its research available to all in the public domain.

After initial operations in La Jolla, California, the laboratories of Dr. Brenner and Dr. Brent joined forces in January of 1998 in over 8,000 square feet of newly-designed and renovated laboratory space in downtown Berkeley, California, one block from the campus of the University of California - Berkeley, and two blocks from its Biology Department.

Institutional relationships

The Institute has initiated affiliations with the University of California - Berkeley and the University of California - San Francisco to enhance its academic environment. Dr. Brenner will be appointed as an Adjunct Professor at UCB and Dr. Brent's appointment as an Adjunct Professor at UCSF is being initiated. The Institute will seek faculty affiliations for future members as appropriate. The Institute hopes to bring together the best elements of a free and innovative academic environment, without the constraints of conventional departmental boundaries.
